text: The surgeon identifies a sigmoid epiploic appendage adhered to the peritoneum medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, preparing to divide it during a robotic inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon assesses the size of the inguinal hernia defect and determines the desired overlap for the mesh. The peritoneal incision is initiated to access the hernia site.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, carefully dissecting between the peritoneum and transversalis fascia. The view transitions to the retro-rectus space, where the inferior epigastric vessels become visible, aiding in the identification of anatomical landmarks for complex inguinal hernia repair.

The surgeon begins by accessing the space of Retzius medially, then moves laterally to divide the layers of the transversalis fascia and investing fascia. The dissection stays close to the peritoneum, allowing the surgeon to navigate around the hernia sac laterally, between the sac and the testicular vessels.

Doing so also prevents me from getting too superficial and injuring the genital femoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. That layer I just opened is what I call the Shiozaki layer. It's always there. It helps get me into the layer where I can separate the fat from the peritoneum. So laterally, there aren't too many structures that can be injured, so I'm usually pretty aggressive. As long as you hug the peritoneum, the genital femoral nerve will be away from you. The only thing to look for are the testicular vessels. In somebody who's had a prostatectomy, this plane is usually a virgin and hasn't been touched. So it's a safe place to start.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery while dissecting near the inferior epigastric vessels, with the left hand retracting tissue to create visible areolar tissue, ensuring the correct plane is maintained. Medially, the dissection is close to the rectus muscle within the space of Retzius.

The surgeon divides the right medial umbilical ligament to create more space for medial dissection. Using the left hand for traction, the surgeon works on reducing the indirect sac, employing short bursts of cautery and pushing to locate the end of the sac.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to maintain hemostasis while carefully dissecting small vessels off the sac. The left hand pulls the tissue to the nine o'clock position, spreading out the layers for better visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and checks its internal location. Short bursts of energy are applied to delineate the different tissue planes, clearly revealing the edge of the sac and the position of the vas deferens.

The surgeon returns to a challenging area, visually identifying landmarks including the vas deferens and inferior epigastric vessels. The surgeon proceeds with more aggressive dissection around the inferior epigastric vessels, while also performing simpler tasks in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract the bladder and fat away from the rectus muscle, revealing white areolar separation planes. The VAS is visible, and the surgeon carefully avoids the inferior epigastric vessels while applying short bursts of energy for dissection.

The surgeon carefully peels away the peritoneum, revealing minimal fat between the peritoneum and pelvic structures, likely due to previous pelvic lymph node dissection. Short bursts of energy are used to visualize tissue planes while avoiding injury to the inferior gastric vessels. As the dissection deepens, the surgeon is cautious of the external iliac vein and femoral vein potentially being pulled medially, searching for fat to ensure these veins are not within the dissection field.

The surgeon prepares to free up the peritoneum and begins dissecting through natural planes between the fat. Scissors are used to push against the tissue, revealing the presence of bone, which helps guide the direction of dissection.

The surgeon identifies a whitish structure as the bladder and proceeds with careful dissection, using sharp dissection and short bursts of cautery to navigate through scar tissue. The surgeon works within and between fatty layers, avoiding excessive energy near the bladder while hugging the bony structures of the pelvis.

The surgeon uses the left hand to retract and reveal a white, foamy areolar layer, demonstrating the fine plane even in dense scar tissue. The right hand precisely cuts through this layer, while dense tissue near the pubis and the bladder are clearly visible. The surgeon pulls on the bladder, emphasizing the importance of hemostasis by stopping any bleeding and using gauze to absorb blood, ensuring clear visibility of tissue planes. As the dissection deepens, attention is paid to the location of the femoral vein.

The surgeon exposes the white medial umbilical ligament and peels the peritoneum away from the testicular vessel to position the mesh posteriorly. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve and the general femoral nerve are visible next to the iliac artery. The surgeon achieves the critical view of the MPO and exchanges the scissors for a mega suture cut needle driver.

The surgeon unfolds a 15x10 piece of anatomical Pritex Pro Grip mesh within the abdomen, ensuring the seam is exposed and positioned approximately two centimeters away from the internal ring for optimal placement.
The surgeon exposes the lower flap and carefully tucks it into the space of Retzius against the pubis and Cooper's ligament, ensuring adequate posterior coverage.

The surgeon checks for any peritoneum behind the posterior flap of the mesh before proceeding with the routine closure. The needle is carefully managed, with the left hand pulling it out and positioning it for the right hand to grasp it for suturing.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 180 V-Loc suture to secure the tissue, making four to six throws before pulling it through. The suture is pulled tight, relaxed, and cut flush to the peritoneum to avoid any tail. A hole in the peritoneum is closed with figure-of-eight 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

And that will complete the operation. The patient will go home in the PACU. I don't make them urinate because I've had zero urinary retention in over a hundred fifty cases so far. They go home with five tablets of Norco. And I tell them to take six hundred milligrams of ibuprofen every six hours and five hundred milligrams of Tylenol every six hours around the clock for the first evening and then as needed. They also go home with Docusate and Senna which I have them start taking immediately and take it until they have a good bowel movement. Thank you for watching.
